title: Benchmarks
intro: A benchmark is a standardized test researchers use to measure how well an AI handles a specific kind of task — solving coding problems, for example, or running terminal commands. You won't find scores here on purpose: they shift almost weekly, and a single benchmark often has several conflicting "official" results in circulation at once. Instead we explain what each benchmark actually measures, and link to its live leaderboard.

benchmarks.name: SWE-bench (+Verified/Pro)
benchmarks.measures: Tests whether an AI agent can resolve real GitHub issues by writing a patch that passes the repository's hidden test suite.
benchmarks.runBy: Originally Princeton & Stanford; the Verified subset was curated with OpenAI, the Pro variant is run by Scale AI.
benchmarks.caveat: Independent audits found solutions that were effectively given away in the issue thread, and tests too weak to catch a broken fix — and SWE-bench Pro alone has several conflicting "official" scores depending on which scaffold and data split is used.

benchmarks.name: Terminal-Bench
benchmarks.measures: Tests whether an agent can complete real multi-step terminal tasks, where every command's output changes what it should do next.
benchmarks.runBy: A Stanford x Laude (Anthropic-adjacent) collaboration, part of the open "Harbor" framework.

benchmarks.name: Aider Polyglot
benchmarks.measures: Tests whether a model follows instructions and edits code correctly, unassisted, across coding exercises in several languages.
benchmarks.runBy: Run by Paul Gauthier, Aider's creator — not a lab or consortium.
benchmarks.caveat: This leaderboard isn't re-run for every new model — it can show outdated models on top for months, simply because newer ones haven't been tested yet.

benchmarks.name: LiveCodeBench
benchmarks.measures: Tests code generation, self-repair, predicting test output, and code execution, using competitive-programming problems pulled in continuously so old problems can't leak into training data.
benchmarks.runBy: Researchers at UC Berkeley, MIT, and Cornell (lead: Naman Jain).

benchmarks.name: LMArena Code Arena / WebDev Arena
benchmarks.measures: Measures human preference, not correctness: real people blind-vote on which of two models' output (code or web-app) they like better.
benchmarks.caveat: A large-scale analysis of vendor submissions found that selectively submitting only the best-performing variant can meaningfully inflate a model's score.

benchmarks.name: HumanEval
benchmarks.measures: Tests functional correctness on hand-written Python programming problems by actually running the generated code against unit tests.
benchmarks.runBy: Created by OpenAI researchers (Chen, Tworek, Jun and others).
benchmarks.caveat: Saturated: nearly all frontier models cluster tightly together at the top, so the benchmark barely tells them apart anymore. It's used only as a basic sanity check now, not for ranking frontier models.

benchmarks.name: Artificial Analysis Coding Agent Index
benchmarks.measures: A composite score for end-to-end coding-agent performance — combining several underlying benchmarks plus cost, token, and time efficiency into one index.
benchmarks.runBy: Run by Artificial Analysis, an independent benchmarking outfit.
benchmarks.caveat: Scores the model-plus-harness combination together, so the same underlying model can rank very differently depending on which agent scaffold runs it.

benchmarks.name: ARC-AGI-2
benchmarks.measures: Tests abstract, fluid reasoning through visual grid-transformation puzzles, plus how efficiently (at what cost) a model solves them. Not a coding benchmark per se.
benchmarks.runBy: Run by ARC Prize Inc., a nonprofit (keeps test data private or semi-private to resist contamination).

benchmarks.name: MMLU / MMLU-Pro
benchmarks.measures: Tests general knowledge and reasoning with multiple-choice questions spanning many academic subjects. Not coding-specific.
benchmarks.runBy: Originally created by Hendrycks and colleagues (UC Berkeley-affiliated researchers).
benchmarks.caveat: Saturated: frontier models cluster too closely together to differentiate. MMLU-Pro was built to fix that, but is reported to be starting to saturate too.

benchmarks.name: METR "Time Horizon"
benchmarks.measures: Tracks how long a task (in human-expert work-time) an agent can complete on its own at a fixed reliability bar — read as a trend line over time, not a single score.
benchmarks.runBy: Run by METR, an independent AI-evaluation nonprofit.
benchmarks.caveat: METR itself flags its measurements for very long tasks as unreliable with the current task suite — read it as a trend line, not a precise cutoff.
